title: All work, no play. The nature of remote engineering.
body: All work, no play. The nature of remote engineering.
pros: Pay and benefits are good, very generous parental leave. (Too bad I wasn't around long enough to take advantage of it) Remote positions available.
cons: It was hard to connect with coworkers due to the remote setting of the job. I learned nothing about what my coworkers are like besides their work antics. Thus there wasn't anyone that would get to joke around with as every Slack message was business. The main reason being that there was so much to do that often times it's a 9-10 hour day due to the need to collaborate between different time zones e.g. 7PM CST = 5PM PST. They won't hesitate to let you go for the benefit of the bottom line during times when the chips are down as a corporation would. The silver lining is that since there was no personal connections made at the company, I felt little when it came time to leave. At least there's severance available when that happens.

title: Legacy Juniper - Was good before being laid off in December
body: Legacy Juniper - Was good before being laid off in December
pros: Juniper had better than average benefits and pay and seemed to be employee focused.
cons: HPEâs benefits are meaningfully worse than Juniperâs, and the integration strategy for the combined product lines remains opaque. There is widespread uncertainty around how overlapping portfoliosâparticularly in switching, Wi-Fi, and WAN/SD-WANâwill be resolved. Despite the long wait for merger approval, there seems to have been limited advance planning for either the products or the organization. Customers who are frustrated by the absence of clear roadmaps shouldnât take it personally; internal HPE teams are largely in the dark too.

title: Many Obstacles, Poor Leadership, Disposable Employees
body: Many Obstacles, Poor Leadership, Disposable Employees
pros: Some of my coworkers at this company are impressive both technically and interpersonally. This is the highlight.
cons: Many simple tasks are difficult due to large corporate overhead and a lot of tech debt. That takes a toll on productivity and the pride of working at HPE. The pattern I've seen is uncontrollable growth, followed by lay offs to protect shareholders. To me this spells poor leadership. There is a noticeable lack of accountability at the VP level and above. The consequences of poor strategic decisions (or lack of) consistently only hurts the individual contributors and middle managers.

title: Great People-Leadership Needs Work
pros: HPE is full of hardworking people who will go out of their way to help each other. The talent level is high, and they truly care about their job. There are some good products from a reliable name.
cons: HPE has had an identity crisis since the Compaq merger and has never really found their niche. Lot's of acquisitions with poor integration and questionable purchases, like the Autonomy scandal, paying $11B for a company worth $2B. There is always water-cooler talk about fearing Mondays in the middle of the quarter as that is when layoffs happen, generally small, rolling reductions, not enough to enact the WARN Act, but still substantial. There have been several travel freezes, constant hiring freezes etc. All the signs of a company trying to "shrink into greatness". It's a good springboard but not somewhere you want to stay more than 3-5 years.

I consider myself incredibly lucky to find this company when I did, and I cannot imagine a scenario where another company would offer me enough to leave.

If you're a software engineer, but you've always wanted to know what it's like to work in a finance team, or a marketing team, or a support team... Here's you chance!
cons: Leadership clearly understands that they need to change to survive. They have goals of changing both the culture and the product delivery method (changing from traditional hardware sales / leases to "as a service" pay by consumption model). They have done a great job in changing the culture, but the transition to hybrid cloud is struggling. The organization as a whole is executing well on their traditional business model, but not executing well on transforming the business model to support the future

body: HPE's not a bad place to work
pros: Well paying Nice office Decent People
cons: HPE is not a bad place to work. Like most other places, it's simply riddled with below-intelligence human beings with basic ineptitude, who are simply trying their best within the means of their limitations.

title: Leave while you can
pros: Great people to work with, good benefits, growth if you work for it, direct management is amazing
cons: This company is a sinking ship. Executive leadership has been tanking the sales organization over the last couple of years with restructuring and WFR's every quarter. You may think you are employed, but in reality you are a month to month contractor. Performance isn't even taken into account for WFR's. You can be a top performer and get canned the next day. It's a shame because they are letting go of great talent. Internal processes are also a disaster and no one seems to know what is going on. If you're a woman prepare to be paid less than your male counterparts, and if you fall victim to any kind of harassments, you will most definitely not be taken care of.

title: If you enjoy ever changing processes this is the place
body: If you enjoy ever changing processes this is the place
pros: Great benefits and probably some other things
cons: Ever-changing processes are constant and confusing. A giant bureaucracy slows everything down. Salaries do not keep up with inflation. HPE is on a constant cycle of 'fundamental transformation' that makes it hard to know what it is the company is selling from day to day. A maddening 'return to office' policy that shoehorns people into a crowded office where they just sub-leased an entire floor forcing everyone even closer together. The open floor plan makes it impossible to get work done quietly.

title: Good Place to work
pros: Hewlett Packard Enterprise (HPE) has been an influential player in the technology industry for many years. Here are some positive aspects commonly associated with working at HPE: Innovation Legacy: HPE has a long history of technological innovation, which can be inspiring for employees who are passionate about technology. Global Reach: Being a global company, HPE offers opportunities to work on international projects and collaborate with colleagues from different cultures and backgrounds. Learning Opportunities: HPE invests in employee training and development, providing access to courses, workshops, and certifications that can boost career growth.
cons: While Hewlett Packard Enterprise (HPE) is a respected player in the technology industry, like any large corporation, it's not immune to challenges and criticisms. Here are some common concerns or negative aspects that have been associated with working at HPE, based on various anecdotal accounts and reviews over the years: Bureaucracy: Due to its size and age, HPE sometimes suffers from bureaucratic processes that can slow down decision-making and innovation. Frequent Reorganizations: Over the years, some employees have expressed concerns about regular company restructures, leading to shifts in team dynamics and even layoffs.

title: Assimilating and dehumanising promising tech companies like The Borg.
body: Assimilating and dehumanising promising tech companies like The Borg.
pros: Interesting technology. Cutting edge. Colleagues and line management are great.
cons: Since HPE everything is behind 300 layers of corporate approval. The startup energy is gone and the job I loved is now one I hate. Getting paid correctly is impossible and no-one cares. For all the corporate BS propaganda thrown at us HPE is a monster buying up energetic start ups and crushing the life out of them with unnecessary layers of management and bureaucracy. 5* for Silver Peak before HPE crushed most of what was good out of it and replaced it with an attitude putting cost cutting first and everything else last even though ex/Aruba is the most profitable part of the business and now subsidising the legacy ones. Presumably as HPE has had more time to pile levels of management and bureaucracy on them and completely choke the life out of them. Aruba it's still a work in progress. Many of us really miss Keerti. All company yes men in charge now.

title: I'm torn. I want to say "Great!" but it also "Sucks!"
body: I'm torn. I want to say "Great!" but it also "Sucks!"
pros: While employed, I had some great experiences with HPE. I learned a whole lot and worked with some very professional people while I was there. I enjoyed the work I did, and I was very dedicated (as I think most people are) to the work they are doing. You will always run into those people who just seem like they are floating by, and somehow keep their jobs. They are frustrating. But besides them, I did learn a lot. I feel like HPE does a fairly good job of making you feel like you are part of something big. They provide you with the tools you need to get the work done. The benefits are pretty good.
cons: I HATE September and October. Every year, you see many great colleagues get laid off. It is a STUPID and TERRIBLE "tradition" of HPE. They give you a 2-week notice, and then you are done. They cut off your benefits and you're done - yet they are still hiring for positions within your organization that you'd be a good fit for. The story they tell you is nonsense: "Your position is no longer needed and the area you have been working is no longer needed" when you are doing work for the things that the company is focused on. While they do provide you with a severance package, it is not enough to get you to the next job if you need to maintain your benefits through COBRA.
role: Former employee - Global Email Marketing Manager

cons: Salary was low, but since you got a car to drive it wasn't too bad. Sometimes you'd have to drive from San Jose to Monterey or Carmel and those were long drives. If you were on-call and had to go out in the middle of the night, those long drives made it even worse.

title: Beware legacy work areas
pros: Sometimes upper level management get things right. Projects are sometimes interesting. Pay and benefits are very good by not crazy like the FAANGs but still very fair. When I worked for HPE the first time it was boring in a good way. No surprises, no weird power trips by managers but I moved to the Cray product line and .... see negatives.
cons: Do NOT go to work for the Cray subdivision. It is absolute governed by fear, abuse, and micromanaging. First line managers fear middle managers and middle managers fear upper levels. The managers are almost uniformly bad. Most are lazy, and many are abusive. I have watched multiple managers scream at engineers for a variety of reasons, and more often the not the managers simply were wrong about facts of the situation. There was a bad agile implementation, that created a lot of friction with no accountability, and somehow upper level management can go down many levels and update an individual Most teams seem to dislike other teams.
